scenarioStory.history: 35-year-old female, 3 weeks postpartum after an uncomplicated cesarean section, with sudden-onset severe shortness of breath and right-sided pleuritic chest pain that started 30 minutes ago while feeding the baby. Has not been very active since the C-section. No cough, no fever, no leg swelling noted but has been sedentary.
scenarioStory.discussion: Recognize massive PE as obstructive shock. Apply MATC Shock protocol for fluid and vasopressor management. Understand why positive pressure ventilation can precipitate arrest in PE. Identify PE risk factors (postpartum, immobility, pregnancy). Use 12-lead EKG findings to support PE diagnosis
scenarioStory.course: Arrival — Postpartum Dyspnea with Chest Pain: Postpartum patient with sudden onset dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hypoxemia, tachycardia, and hypotension. Clear lungs and risk factor profile raise strong suspicion for massive PE. This is obstructive shock physiology. 12-lead may show S1Q3T3, right heart strain, sinus tachycardia, or a new RBBB. → Obstructive Shock Management: Patient remains hypotensive despite initial fluid bolus. Crew escalates to push-dose epinephrine or vasopressor infusion while continuing supportive care and expedited transport.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Sudden severe shortness of breath and chest pain

scenarioEvents.name: Arrival — Postpartum Dyspnea with Chest Pain
scenarioEvents.description: Postpartum patient with sudden onset dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hypoxemia, tachycardia, and hypotension. Clear lungs and risk factor profile raise strong suspicion for massive PE. This is obstructive shock physiology. 12-lead may show S1Q3T3, right heart strain, sinus tachycardia, or a new RBBB.

scenarioEvents.name: Obstructive Shock Management
scenarioEvents.description: Patient remains hypotensive despite initial fluid bolus. Crew escalates to push-dose epinephrine or vasopressor infusion while continuing supportive care and expedited transport.

checklist.title: Recognize the pattern: postpartum, sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, hypoxemia, hypotension, clear lungs, low EtCO2 = PE
Obtain 12-lead EKG for right heart strain
Administer judicious fluid bolus 500 mL NS with reassessment
Initiate vasopressor (norepinephrine infusion or push-dose epinephrine) for shock
Expedited transport to a facility with advanced PE therapies
Apply cardiac monitor and waveform capnography
If deterioration to arrest: high-quality CPR and consider prolonged resuscitation
